3: Climate change has become a significant challenge for humans nowadays (Gan et al., 2011). The concentration of carbon dioxide (CO2) in the atmosphere has increased from approximately 277 ppm in 1750 (Joos and Spahni, 2008) to 405 ppm in 2017 (Tigchelaar et al., 2018). Global anthropogenic greenhouse gases (GHG) emissions increase remarkably at 53.5 Gt CO2-eq (WMO, 2018). As a result, extreme climate events emerged frequently during the past decades over the world, including declined yields of crops (Zhao et al., 2017) and fisheries (Moore et al., 2018), biodi- versity loss (Urban, 2015), rising sea levels (Cazenave and Le Cozannet, 2014), etc. Climate change will be possibly long-lasting and irreversible even if the GHG emissions would be possibly

slow down (Solomon et al., 2009), affecting human life and global ecosystems. Therefore, effective practices have to be adopted in different sectors and regions for reducing global GHG emissions from human activities.

Livestock production makes up a significant contribution to GHG emissions (Buratti et al., 2017). The global livestock sector contributes to 7.1 Gt CO2-eq GHG emissions each year, representing 14.5% of human-induced GHG emissions in the world (Gerber et al., 2013). During the past twenty years, meat consumption in the world has been increased by 56.59%. OECD (2019) predicted that the growth in meat consumption would expand by 40 Mt in 2028 because of a combination growth of population and income, especially in Asian and Latin American countries. The rapid growth of requirements for animal products will possibly trigger more GHG emissions in the future.
Pig production system provides more than one-third of the meat products in the world (Zhu and Chen, 2018). Meanwhile, it is esti- mated to cause about 668 Mt of CO2-eq GHG emissions per year.

China, as the largest producer and consumer of pork in the world, produced approximately half of the global pork production (NBS, 2019). With the rapid growth of Chinese income, the consump- tion amount of pork in China is projected to be 58.05 Mt in 2028 (OECD, 2019). As a consequence, the production quantity of pig in China is expected to be steadily increased in the future. The rapid development of the pig production sector requires more industrial inputs and generates more excreta, which will possibly aggravate the impact of the Chinese pig production sector on global climate change. Therefore, it has become increasingly important to un- derstand the general characteristics and key points of GHG emis- sions from pig production in China. This contributes to providing potential solutions to reducing the anthropogenic GHG emissions of the livestock sector in the world.

Presently, there have been many studies assessing the GHG emissions of the pig production sector in Argentina (Andretta al., 2018), Brazil (Arrieta and González, 2019), Canada (Verge et al., 2009), France (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005) and so forth. In recent years, some researchers also paid attention to GHG emissions in pig production in China. For example, Luo et al. (2015) quantified the CF of pig production in households and aggregated farms in Sichuan province, China. Wang et al. (2017) estimated the GHG emissions from enteric fermentation and manure manage- ment of China pig production sector from 1960 to 2010. Zhou et al. (2018) explored potential GHG mitigation roads of the large-scale pig production system by analyzing a typical pig farm in northern China. Xue et al. (2019) applied a life cycle assessment (LCA) approach to estimate the potential GHG emissions of pig farming in Liaoning province, China. These studies provide valuable cases to grasp the GHG emissions characteristics of China's pig industry. However, the published studies just focused on specific pig pro- duction models in China. The total GHG emissions derived pig production in China and the detailed assessment of the uncertainty has not been reported at present. Also, the possible trend of the GHG emissions of pig sectors in China in the future has not been predicted. In other words, the whole picture of GHG sources and reduction points of pig production in China has not been compre- hensively described nowadays.

CF is an effective method to quantify the GHG emissions derived from products and to identify the key points of GHG reduction of them. The methodology refers to calculating the sum of GHG emissions in the production system based on the LCA method and evaluating the impact on climate change in the form of CO2 equivalents (ISO, The CF has been widely applied to quantify the global warming potential (GWP) of various agricultural sys- items, for instance, fruits and vegetable production and retail in Swiss (Stoessel et al., 2012), wheat production involving different farming practices in Canada (Gan et al., 2014), primary crops and fruits production in China (Wang et al., 2019a), etc. During the past decades, the effects of livestock production systems on the global climate change have been evaluated in different regions by using the CF method, including the livestock production system in the European Union (Lesschen et al., 2011), the grazing goat system in a natural park in southern Spain (Gutiérrez-Peña et al., 2019), milk production in Sub-Saharan Africa (Wilkes et al., 2020). To our knowledge, however, the CF has not been used for quantifying the GHG emissions of pig production systems in China at a national level.

Therefore, this study introduced the CF method to analyze the GHG emissions derived from pig production system in China based on the national statistical data from 2000 to 2016. Specifically, the objectives in this work were as follows: (1) quantifying the CF of unit alive pig in different breeding models in China; (2) presenting the historical dynamic of total GHG emissions from pig sector in China; (3) identifying the hot spots of reducing GHG emissions of

pig production process in China based on the LCA approach; (4) estimating the possible quantity of GHG emissions of Chinese pig sector in the future by scenario analysis. (5) employing Monte Carlo simulation (MCS) to trace the uncertainty in parameters. This study would contribute to the development of the pig production system in China with a cleaner production approach. Meanwhile, it would provide valuable information for understanding the GHG emissions feature of the global livestock sector.

Presently, there have been many studies assessing the GHG emissions of the pig production sector in Argentina (Andretta al., 2018), Brazil (Arrieta and González, 2019), Canada (Verge et al., 2009), France (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005) and so forth. In recent years, some researchers also paid attention to GHG emissions in pig production in China. For example, Luo et al. (2015) quantified the CF of pig production in households and aggregated farms in Sichuan province, China. Wang et al. (2017) estimated the GHG emissions from enteric fermentation and manure manage- ment of China pig production sector from 1960 to 2010. Zhou et al. (2018) explored potential GHG mitigation roads of the large-scale pig production system by analyzing a typical pig farm in northern China. Xue et al. (2019) applied a life cycle assessment (LCA) approach to estimate the potential GHG emissions of pig farming in Liaoning province, China. These studies provide valuable cases to grasp the GHG emissions characteristics of China's pig industry. However, the published studies just focused on specific pig pro- duction models in China. The total GHG emissions derived pig production in China and the detailed assessment of the uncertainty has not been reported at present. Also, the possible trend of the GHG emissions of pig sectors in China in the future has not been predicted. In other words, the whole picture of GHG sources and reduction points of pig production in China has not been compre- hensively described nowadays.

CF is an effective method to quantify the GHG emissions derived from products and to identify the key points of GHG reduction of them. The methodology refers to calculating the sum of GHG emissions in the production system based on the LCA method and evaluating the impact on climate change in the form of CO2 equivalents (ISO, The CF has been widely applied to quantify the global warming potential (GWP) of various agricultural sys- items, for instance, fruits and vegetable production and retail in Swiss (Stoessel et al., 2012), wheat production involving different farming practices in Canada (Gan et al., 2014), primary crops and fruits production in China (Wang et al., 2019a), etc. During the past decades, the effects of livestock production systems on the global climate change have been evaluated in different regions by using the CF method, including the livestock production system in the European Union (Lesschen et al., 2011), the grazing goat system in a natural park in southern Spain (Gutiérrez-Peña et al., 2019), milk production in Sub-Saharan Africa (Wilkes et al., 2020). To our knowledge, however, the CF has not been used for quantifying the GHG emissions of pig production systems in China at a national level.

Therefore, this study introduced the CF method to analyze the GHG emissions derived from pig production system in China based on the national statistical data from 2000 to 2016. Specifically, the objectives in this work were as follows: (1) quantifying the CF of unit alive pig in different breeding models in China; (2) presenting the historical dynamic of total GHG emissions from pig sector in China; (3) identifying the hot spots of reducing GHG emissions of

pig production process in China based on the LCA approach; (4) estimating the possible quantity of GHG emissions of Chinese pig sector in the future by scenario analysis. (5) employing Monte Carlo simulation (MCS) to trace the uncertainty in parameters. This study would contribute to the development of the pig production system in China with a cleaner production approach. Meanwhile, it would provide valuable information for understanding the GHG emissions feature of the global livestock sector.
8: Climate change has become a significant challenge for humans nowadays (Gan et al., 2011). The concentration of carbon dioxide (CO2) in the atmosphere has increased from approximately 277 ppm in 1750 (Joos and Spahni, 2008) to 405 ppm in 2017 (Tigchelaar et al., 2018). Global anthropogenic greenhouse gases (GHG) emissions increase remarkably at 53.5 Gt CO2-eq (WMO, 2018). As a result, extreme climate events emerged frequently during the past decades over the world, including declined yields of crops (Zhao et al., 2017) and fisheries (Moore et al., 2018), biodi- versity loss (Urban, 2015), rising sea levels (Cazenave and Le Cozannet, 2014), etc. Climate change will be possibly long-lasting and irreversible even if the GHG emissions would be possibly

slow down (Solomon et al., 2009), affecting human life and global ecosystems. Therefore, effective practices have to be adopted in different sectors and regions for reducing global GHG emissions from human activities.

Livestock production makes up a significant contribution to GHG emissions (Buratti et al., 2017). The global livestock sector contributes to 7.1 Gt CO2-eq GHG emissions each year, representing 14.5% of human-induced GHG emissions in the world (Gerber et al., 2013). During the past twenty years, meat consumption in the world has been increased by 56.59%. OECD (2019) predicted that the growth in meat consumption would expand by 40 Mt in 2028 because of a combination growth of population and income, especially in Asian and Latin American countries. The rapid growth of requirements for animal products will possibly trigger more GHG emissions in the future.
Pig production system provides more than one-third of the meat products in the world (Zhu and Chen, 2018). Meanwhile, it is esti- mated to cause about 668 Mt of CO2-eq GHG emissions per year.

China, as the largest producer and consumer of pork in the world, produced approximately half of the global pork production (NBS, 2019). With the rapid growth of Chinese income, the consump- tion amount of pork in China is projected to be 58.05 Mt in 2028 (OECD, 2019). As a consequence, the production quantity of pig in China is expected to be steadily increased in the future. The rapid development of the pig production sector requires more industrial inputs and generates more excreta, which will possibly aggravate the impact of the Chinese pig production sector on global climate change. Therefore, it has become increasingly important to un- derstand the general characteristics and key points of GHG emis- sions from pig production in China. This contributes to providing potential solutions to reducing the anthropogenic GHG emissions of the livestock sector in the world.

Presently, there have been many studies assessing the GHG emissions of the pig production sector in Argentina (Andretta al., 2018), Brazil (Arrieta and González, 2019), Canada (Verge et al., 2009), France (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005) and so forth. In recent years, some researchers also paid attention to GHG emissions in pig production in China. For example, Luo et al. (2015) quantified the CF of pig production in households and aggregated farms in Sichuan province, China. Wang et al. (2017) estimated the GHG emissions from enteric fermentation and manure manage- ment of China pig production sector from 1960 to 2010. Zhou et al. (2018) explored potential GHG mitigation roads of the large-scale pig production system by analyzing a typical pig farm in northern China. Xue et al. (2019) applied a life cycle assessment (LCA) approach to estimate the potential GHG emissions of pig farming in Liaoning province, China. These studies provide valuable cases to grasp the GHG emissions characteristics of China's pig industry. However, the published studies just focused on specific pig pro- duction models in China. The total GHG emissions derived pig production in China and the detailed assessment of the uncertainty has not been reported at present. Also, the possible trend of the GHG emissions of pig sectors in China in the future has not been predicted. In other words, the whole picture of GHG sources and reduction points of pig production in China has not been compre- hensively described nowadays.

CF is an effective method to quantify the GHG emissions derived from products and to identify the key points of GHG reduction of them. pig production process in China based on the LCA approach; (4) estimating the possible quantity of GHG emissions of Chinese pig sector in the future by scenario analysis. (5) employing Monte Carlo simulation (MCS) to trace the uncertainty in parameters. 9: <header>2. Methods and data </header>
2.1. System description of pig production in China

The pig production system in China could be classified into four models according to its breeding scale (Table 1), including backyard farm (BF), specialized farm (SF), medium-scale farm (MF) and large- scale farm (LF) (EBCAHVY, 2016). Table 1 presents the basic infor- mation on the four pig production models. The BF and SF could be viewed as household farms, while the MF and LF could be classified as the large-scale pig husbandry models. Here, according to the definition principle of productivity systems which provided by IPCC (2019) and the system description of each pig production model in China, BF and SF model were considered as low productivity sys- items, while MF and LF model were considered as high productivity systems in this study. The changed amounts on inputs and outputs in the models affected the GHG emissions during the pig produc- tion process.

The BF and SF model both normally own farmland to plant a part of feedstuffs such as maize for pig rearing. The MF and LF belong to intensive rearing farms, so they normally purchase raw feedstuffs and mix them based on suitable proportion or directly purchase commercial mixed feed. Regardless of the feed sources, the in- gredients of pig feeds are generally similar, including maize, wheat bran, rice hull, soybean meal, cotton seed meal, reap seed meal, vitamin premix, etc., only are the proportion of feed ingredients differently. Generally, maize makes up about 70% of pig feeds in China (AHDB, 2018), followed by the wheat brain (Hu and Wang, 2015). Because there was no standard feed formula for pig rear- ing in actual productive condition, the inventory of pig feed in this study was analyzed based on the common formula of pig feed provided by Chen and Yang (2013). The details on feed composition were showed in the supplementary material (Table S1). Moreover, green feeds (e.g. weeds and crop byproducts) and wasted foods are always used as pig feeds in China, especially in the BF and SF models. The feedstuffs were not considered in the case for two reasons. On the one hand, naturally grown weed did not generate anthropogenic GHG emissions. On the other hand, the environ- mental impacts from crop byproducts and wasted foods were assumed to be zero as it was wastes produced by other systems.

Energy and labor consumption are important sources of GHG emissions during the pig rearing period. Different energy types and quantities were used in each model in the breeding process in practical production. Therein, heat preservation, cooling, ventila- tion, feed processing would create the consumption of coal, elec- tricity and oil. The consumption of the labor force was mainly generated in the farm management. The evident difference be- tween the models on the consumed amount of energy and labor could be observed in Table 1.

Moreover, manure management is the key point of GHG emis- sions in pig production as well. Manure management practices in different farms are various, including washing, dry collection, solid storage, fermentation and so on. In recent years, the Chinese gov- ernment has vigorously promoted to the recycling use of animal manure. A few large-scale pig rearing farms have applied the advanced practices for pig manure management. At present,
Table 1 Productive features of four pig production systems in this study.

Model|Rearing scale a (heads)|Period b (days)|Slaughter weight b (kg/head)|Output quantity C (million head/year)|Feed consumption d (kg/head)|Electricity (kWh/head)|Coal (kg/ head)|Labor ( person/ head/yr )|Manure management g BF|1
a Obtained from China animal husbandry and veterinary yearbook (EBCAHVY, 2016).
b	Average breeding periods and slaughter weights of pig in different models during 2000-2016 (DPNDRC, 2016).
C	Average output quantity of pig in different models during 2000-2016 (NBS, 2016).
d	Derived from DPNDRC (2016), excluding the green feeds and wasted foods.
e	Average consumed amounts of electricity and coal for pig production during 2000-2016.
f Obtained from DPNDRC (2016).
g Derived from Zhu et al. (2020).

nevertheless, the main management system of pig manure in China was dry collection (>80%), solid storage and return to farmland (>50%) (Zhu et al., 2020). Therefore, this study calculated the GHG emissions of pig manure management based on the dry collection and solid storage methods. The field application of pig manure after solid storage were not considered during the CF accounting because it was excluded from the system boundary in this study. Generally, the CFs accounting of the four models could reflect the general characteristic of GHG emissions of pig production in China and provide a complete picture to understand the effects of Chinese pig production sector on global climate change.

14: The system boundary in this study consisted of three sub- systems (Fig. 1), including (1) CF accounting of unit pig in different rearing models, (2) total GHG emissions from pig production sys- tem in China, and (3) scenario analysis of the possible GHG emis- sions of Chinese pig sector in 2030. The GHG considered in this study included CO2, CH4, N20. The 100-year GWP of CH4 and N20 are 28 and 265 times the intensity of CO2 on a mass basis (Myhre et al., 2013).

The CF accounting scope of unit alive pig ranged from feedstuffs production to farm gate in this study. The GHG emissions in pig production were primarily affected by four aspects, including feeds consumption, enteric fermentation and manure management, farm management. Therein, the feeds consumption could be divided to feed crop cultivation and feeds processing. The subsequent pro- cesses, such as slaughtering, processing, packing, were excluded in the present case. Thus, the function unit selected in this subsystem was kg live weight pig. Then, based on the CF accounting results of producing unit alive pig in different models, the total GHG emis- sions of pig production system in China during 2000-2016 were quantified. Finally, on the basis of the key points of reducing GHG emissions of pig production in China, this study intended to esti- mate the GHG emissions of the pig production system in China in 2030 under different scenarios.

It should be noted that the rearing of sows, piglets and breeding boar were not taken into account in the system boundary of the research due to unavailable data. The transportation process linking all production steps was not taken into account in the study as well, because the transportation approaches and distances associated with the pig production in different models and different regions were various, leading to the unavailable data sources.
15: The system boundary in this study consisted of three sub- systems (Fig. 1), including (1) CF accounting of unit pig in different rearing models, (2) total GHG emissions from pig production sys- tem in China, and (3) scenario analysis of the possible GHG emis- sions of Chinese pig sector in 2030. The GHG considered in this study included CO2, CH4, N20. The 100-year GWP of CH4 and N20 are 28 and 265 times the intensity of CO2 on a mass basis (Myhre et al., 2013).

The CF accounting scope of unit alive pig ranged from feedstuffs production to farm gate in this study. The GHG emissions in pig production were primarily affected by four aspects, including feeds consumption, enteric fermentation and manure management, farm management. Therein, the feeds consumption could be divided to feed crop cultivation and feeds processing. The subsequent pro- cesses, such as slaughtering, processing, packing, were excluded in the present case. Thus, the function unit selected in this subsystem was kg live weight pig. Then, based on the CF accounting results of producing unit alive pig in different models, the total GHG emis- sions of pig production system in China during 2000-2016 were quantified. Finally, on the basis of the key points of reducing GHG emissions of pig production in China, this study intended to esti- mate the GHG emissions of the pig production system in China in 2030 under different scenarios.

It should be noted that the rearing of sows, piglets and breeding boar were not taken into account in the system boundary of the research due to unavailable data. The transportation process linking all production steps was not taken into account in the study as well, because the transportation approaches and distances associated with the pig production in different models and different regions were various, leading to the unavailable data sources.

In this study, a LCA-based CF approach was applied to calculate

the GHG emissions of unit pig in different models in China. Therefore, the CFs of unit pig in different models were calculated based on the equation (Eq. (1)): + CFFP,i,y + CFFM,i,y CFENF,,, CFMM,i,y (1) Wpiiy

where i referred to the pig rearing model, y meant the year; CFi,y was the CF of the unit alive pig in the ith model in the yth year (kg CO2-eq/kg); CFFC,i,y, CFFP,i,y, CFFM,i,y, CFENF,,,, CFMM,i,y represented the CF of unit live pig in feedstuffs production, feed processing, farm management, enteric fermentation and manure management in the ith model in the yth year (kg CO2-eq/head/yr), respectively; Wp,i,y was the slaughter weight of unit pig in the ith model in the yth year (kg live weight/head).
18: The upstream GHG emissions resulted from feed crop cultiva- tion was calculated in this study. Only were the main feed in- gredients, including maize, wheat brain and soybean meal, considered for the CF accounting of an alive pig, because the remaining ingredients accounted for less than 5% of the feed for- mula (Table S1). Moreover, as the wheat bran and soybean meal were the byproducts of wheat production and soybean oil pro- duction systems, their GHG emissions factors should be deter- mined by allocation coefficients. In the present study, the energy allocation approach was selected to calculate the allocation co- efficients of wheat bran and soybean meal. Therefore, the CFFC,i,y (kg CO2-eq/head) was calculated according to the equation (Eq. (2)): CFrciy=(Wmiy XEFmy) + (Wwijx EFwy X EAwy, (2)

where Wm,i,y, Ww,i,y and Ws,i,y were the consumed amount of maize, wheat brain, soybean meal by unit pig in the ith model in the yth year (kg/head), respectively, which were obtained from the na- tional statistics data in China (DPNDRC, 2016); EFw,y and EFs,y were the CF factors of maize, wheat and soybean in the yth year, respectively, which was taken from the previous study (Wang et al., 2019a) (supplementary material Table S2); EAw,y and EAs,y were the energy allocation coefficient of the wheat brain and soybean meal in the yth year (%), respectively; The details of the calculation process and correlative parameters were shown in the supple- mentary material (Table S3).

24: A large amount of GHG emissions in livestock production sys- tem was generally derived from the process of composting manure. The majority of pig production farm collect manure from pig house each 1-2 days, then the collected manure is transferred to solid storage step immediately, so the GHG emissions from the dry collection step can be neglected during the GHG emissions ac- counting. The field application of pig manure after solid storage were not considered because it was excluded from the system boundary in this study. As a result, the GHG emissions accounting in this case primarily focused on the solid storage step of manure management system of pig production. In this study, CFMM,i,y was the GHG inventories regarding manure management of unit pig in
the ith model in the yth year (kg CO2-eq/kg), which was calculated according to IPCC (2019) by using the following equation (Eq. (6)): CFMM,i,y = CH4,i,y 28 + (N2OD,iy+N2O1D,iy) 265 (6)

where CH4,i,y referred as the CH4 emission of unit pig from manure management in the ith model in the yth year (kg CH4/head); N2O and N2OID,i,y were the direct and indirect N20 emissions from manure management system (kg N2O/head); 28 and 256 were the global warming potentials of CH4 and N20.

29: Based on the CFs accounting of the unit pig in China in 2016, we intended to estimate the GHG emissions of the pig production system in China in 2030. Therefore, this study sets five possible scenarios of affecting the GHG emissions of crop system in China based on the key factors of affecting GHG emissions of in Chinese pig sector, including changed demand for pork (Scenarios A1 and A2), advanced rearing technologies (Scenarios B1 and B2), and changed rearing model (Scenario C). All assumptions in the sce- narios were designed according to the official policies in China and projections from international organizations (Table The detailed calculation process and relevant parameters were presented in the supplementary material (Table S12-13).
30: The demand for pork in China is positively related to the pop- ulation. As predicted by PDDESA (2019) the Chinese population in 2030 would be 1.46 billion. In the scenario A1, we assumed that the pork self-efficiency would be maintained at an expected goal of 95% (GOSC, 2019). As a result, the required quantity of pig in China would reach approximately 693.03 million heads.
31: There is still a big gap between China's per capita meat con- sumption and that of some developed countries nowadays. Based on data from the OECD/FAO (2019), we assumed that the per capita pork consumption of Chinese would increase from the current level (31.201 rwe/cap/yr) to the developed country level (43.07 kg rwe/ cap/yr). Therefore, the demand for pig numbers was estimated to be 923.83 million heads under the scenario A2.
32: <header>2.5.3. Scenario B1: improved feed conversion rate </header>

Increasing the feed conversion rate is an effective approach to reduce GHG emissions, which effectively promote to reduce feed intake and shorten the breeding period. The Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs in China issued the "National pig production and

development plan (2016-2020)", indicating that the feed conver- sion rate should be improved by 2.9:1 for fattening pigs in 2020 (MOA, 2016). However, the conversion rate of pig feed in China was still higher than 3.2:1 (Hu and Wang, 2015) at present. Accordingly, this scenario assumed that the feed conversation ratio would be the American level as 2.72:1 (AHDB, 2018) due to improved technolo- gies in 2030. Accordingly, the feed conversion rate of pig produc- tion in China would be expected to increase by 15% compared to that in 2016. In other words, under the B1 scenario, the feed quantity of unit pig in each model was reduced by 15%. As a result, the demand for pig numbers was estimated to be 669.23 million heads under the scenario B1.
33: The comprehensive utilization rate of pig manure was less than half of the total production amount nowadays in China (MOA, 2016). In recent years, the Chinese government issued a series of policies to promote treating the livestock manure by biogas project (MOA, 2018a, b). It has been viewed as an effective approach for reducing the GHG emissions of animal rearing. In the scenario B2, therefore, we assumed that all manure in the pig sector in China would be collected and treated by the biogas project in 2030. To be specific, pig manure would be produced and subsequently used in biogas production. The accounting framework and factors used in the scenario B2 were derived from the Clean Development Mech- anism (CDM) methodology AMS-III.D (CDM, 2009). The detailed calculation process of GHG emissions of manure management un- der scenario B2 was presented in the supplementary material (Table S14). Under this scenario, the GHG emissions produced by manure management system would turn into the source of po- tential reduction. Based on this assumption, we could estimate the possible reduction effect of the GHG emissions in Chinese pig sectors under the improved manure management system.
34: <header>2.5.5. Scenario C: expanding scale of pig rearing farms </header>

The pig rearing scale in China has been constantly expanding. The share of scale farming (head>500) would become the main rearing model in China according to the policy suggestion issued by the Chinese government (MOA, 2016). In this scenario, therefore, we assumed that the BF and SF models would be completely replaced by the MF and LF models, because the MF and LF were expected to be the primary production model in China in the future. As a consequence, the share of MF and LF models were assumed to be 81% and 19%, respectively, in 2030 in China. This ratio was estimated based on the proportion of MF and LF in 2016. According to the assumption, we could estimate the possible reduction effect of the GHG emissions in Chinese pig sector when the pig rearing model would be expanded due to market competition and gov- ernment guidance.
Potential scenarios affecting the GHG emissions of the pig production system in China.

36: Uncertainty analysis is a systematic procedure to quantify the uncertainty introduced in the results of a life cycle inventory analysis due to the cumulative effects of model imprecision, input uncertainty and data variability (ISO, 2006). The uncertainties associated with the model predictions of livestock production were generally derived from input data, parameters, model scope, allo- cation methods and the modelling approach (Roos and Nylinder, 2013). In this study, the model scope, modelling approach and allocation methods were determined according to our research objective and IPCC's guidelines. Meanwhile, the raw input data of each pig production in each year were identified, as the official statistical data were used in the case. Thus, the uncertainties in this research were primarily derived from the parameter.

Monte Carlo simulation (MCS) has been identified as the most widely used uncertainty propagation method in the area of LCA and CF in livestock systems (Groen et al., 2017). Hence, it was also applied in this study. The parameter uncertainties are the mean uncertainties related to input data which possibly affect the simulation results with significant level. In general, the parameters are described by a probability distribution in the MCS. The basic principle of defining probability distributions for input parameter were illustrated in supplementary material (Table S15). Depending upon the number of uncertainties and ranges for the parameters, the MCS would conduct iterations of model calculations by using a different set of random values from the probability functions each time. Therefore, the results of MCS consist of a number of possible outcomes of the calculation, giving a representation of the proba- bility of different results based on the uncertainty and variation in the input data (Harrison et al., 2010).

This study run the MCS for the CF of unit live weight in each model and for the total GHG emissions of pig production system in China. The values of the variables were firstly determined according to the default values and their lower and upper limit values in IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006; IPCC, 2019). Then, the standard variance of the following parameters to identity its distribution type. The values and descriptions for parameter that contributed to uncer- tainty were presented in supplementary material (Table S16).

The software package "Crystal Ball" was used to run the MCS in this study. The model was calculated for 10,000 repeats. The simulation would generate a range of results based on different input values of the variables, which presented as histogram. The results contributed to understanding the impact from uncertainties in those parameters.

39: 3.1. Total GHG emissions of pig production system in China

As shown in Table 3, a steady reduction of total GHG emissions of the pig production system in China was presented from 2000 to 2007, with a decreased percentage of 19.49%. Then, a general up- ward trend was witnessed in the following years. In 2014, the total GHG emissions showed the highest value during the study period. Specifically, the BF contributed to the largest share of total GHG emissions of the pig sector before 2010, but the proportion had a significant reduction over the research period. The GHG emissions from the BF in 2016 decreased by 86.40% compared to that in 2000. In contrast, the GHG emissions from the SF, MF, and LF models were remarkably arisen in the study period. The percentage of GHG emissions from the SF, MF, and LF were increased from 6.03%, 7.81%, and 3.10% in 2000 to 24.93%, 37.32% and 26.34% in 2016, respectively.
Generally, total GHG emissions from the Chinese pig sector has
Table 3 The annual total CF of pig production in different models from 2000 to 2016.

decreased from 128.37 Mt CO2-eq in 2000 to 127.08 Mt CO2-eq in 2016, presenting a slight downward percentage of 1.00%. However, the pig production quantity was significantly increased by 35.12% during the study period. Therefore, the results could demonstrate that variations of GHG emissions from the Chinese pig sector were mainly driven by the changed pig rearing models during the past year. In other words, the transformation of the breeding model from extensive household farms to intensive scale farms contrib- uted to the reduction of GHG emissions of the pig production system in China.

43: <header>3.2. Dynamic change of the CF for unit pig production </header>

From 2000 to 2016, the CF of unit mass alive pig in the four models all presented an obvious decline trend (Fig. 2). Therein, the CF in the BF dropped the most, decreasing by 28.09% during the research period. The values in the SF, MF, and LF reduced by 11.89%, 22.64% and 18.35%, respectively, during 2000-2016. Nevertheless, the BF still maintained the highest CF for producing unit pig in the past years. In 2016, the CF of unit pig in the BF, SF, MF and LF were 1.78 kg CO2-eq/kg/yr, 1.55 kg CO2-eq/kg/yr, 1.65 kg CO2-eq/kg/yr and 1.65 kg CO2-eq/kg/yr, respectively. The results presented that different rearing models affected the GHG emissions of pig pro- duction in China. The BF was a high GHG emissions model compared to other models in China.
44: Fig. 3 shows the contribution of production stages to the GHG emissions of pig production in China. During the research period, feed crop cultivation has been the primary contributor to the GHG emissions of pig production, increasing from 34.96% in 2000 to 47.18% in 2016. Manure management basically made up the second- largest contributor to the GHG emissions of pig production. Also, its contribution share has grown from 15.07% in 2000 to 24.87% in 2016. Moreover, the contribution of the farm management changed sharply, decreasing from 37.01% in 2000 to 7.48% in 2016. The GHG emissions from enteric fermentation and feed processing have not been appearing to be dominant in the research period. In general, the results showed that the feeds input and manure management were the key points of reducing GHG emissions of pig production in China.

46: Five scenarios and the interactions of their combinations were considered to estimate the GHG emissions from the pig production system in China in 2030 (Fig. 4). Evidently, there was a wide range between these results, ranging from 18.48 to 176.42 Mt CO2-eq/yr. Among five simulation scenarios, the scenario B2 has the best GHG emissions reduction effect (34.72 Mt CO2-eq/yr) compared with the other single factor scenarios while the scenario A2 showed the highest GHG emissions (170.36 Mt CO2-eq/yr). The GHG emissions under the scenarios A1, B1 and C were 127.80, 107.82, 121.84 Mt CO2-eq/yr, respectively.

When considering the interactions of the scenarios, the scenario A1 X A2 would reach about 176.42 Mt CO2-eq, producing the highest GHG emissions. The scenario A1 X B2 X C had a remarkable GHG emissions reduction effect, presenting an amount of 18.48 Mt

CO2-eq. Furthermore, excluding the scenarios A1, A2, A1 X A2, A1 X C, A1 X A2 X B1, A1 X A2 X C and A1 X A2 X B1 X C, the other scenarios also showed different degrees in GHG emissions reduc- tion compared to that in 2016.

The results illustrated that population growth and rising de- mand were important factors resulting in the growth of GHG emissions from the Chinese pig sector. The combination of expanding pig breeding scale, improved feed conversion rate and manure management would be the greatest effective approach to reduce the GHG emissions of pig production systems in China.

50: 4.1. Historical dynamics and the future estimate of GHG emissions from pig production in China

There has been a lot of literature indicating that livestock pro- duction is closely associated with GHG emissions (Savian et al., 2014). In this study, we found that the total GHG emissions from the Chinese pig sector has been 127.08 Mt CO2-eq in 2016. The value presented a slight downward percentage of 1.00% compared to that in 2000. Standing at the point of total GHG emissions, it could conclude that the pig production system in China did not show an obvious reduced effect on GHG during the past years. Nowadays, China has been one of the largest contributors to global GHG emissions. In 2017, the agricultural GHG emissions in China have been about 0.68 billion tons CO2-eq, ranking the first in the world (FAO, 2020). A previous study in our research group indicated that crop production pushes up GHG emissions in China during 2000-2016 (Wang et al., 2019a). The Chinese government prom- ised to reach the highest point of GHG emissions in 2030 through diversified technologies for reducing emissions. Therefore, Chinese agricultural systems, including crop and livestock production, should pay more attention to exploring GHG reduction practices.
Nevertheless, a fact must be noticed: the pig production quan- tity in China was increased by 35.12% during 2000-2016. In other words, the GHG emissions of producing unit pig in China were

significantly decreased in the past years. The primary reason causing the result was the changed pig rearing models. From 2000 to 2016, the pig production quantity from the BF decreased by 80.90%, while the quantities from the SF, MF and LF increased by 316.63%, 444.15%, 798.08%, respectively. Our results demonstrated that the pig's CF in the BF has been higher than other models in China during the research period, especially before 2004. Thus, the expansion of intensive pig production farms including the SF, MF and LF actually reflected the adoption of pig rearing models with the lower CFs in China. Similar viewpoints have been reported before. Crosson et al. (2011) indicated that intensification of pro- duction could reduce emissions/kg product when input re- quirements of feed and/or fertilizer were not excessive. Dourmad et al. (2014) demonstrated that the degree of intensification inversely correlated with the environmental impact per kg of live pig produced. Qian et al. (2018) indicated that the animals raised with large-scale feedlots practice generated fewer pollutants than those raised in households by investigating the environmental status of livestock and poultry sectors in China. The researches illustrated that the transformation of the breeding model from extensive household farms to intensive scale farms contributed to the reduction of GHG emissions of the pig production system in China. The policy issued by the Chinese government (MOA, 2016) addressed that the share of scale farming (head>500) would be expected to become the main model of the pig production system in China. The simulated results of scenario C would decrease at 123.66 Mt CO2-eq/yr in 2030 with certain emissions reduction potential. According to the results in this paper, the policy will be worthy to be encouraged in the future from the point of reducing GHG emissions. However, the livestock production scale must be moderated (Wang et al., 2016). Pan et al. (2019) indicated that intensification and pollutant nutrient emissions showed an inver- ted U-shaped relationship. Thus, the pig production scale in China would not show the fact of "the larger, the better".

Moreover, we found that the CF of unit pig in BF, SF, MF and LF were declined from 2000 to 2016 in this study (Fig. 2). Neverthe- less, the annual pig production quantity was increased significantly in the research period. The increased amount of the total GHG emissions caused by the growth of annual pig production quantity was larger than the reduction of GHG emissions caused by the decreased CF of pig production, so the total GHG emissions of pig production system presented an upward trend after 2004 (Table 3). Overall, we could conclude that the pig production system in China had developed toward a positive effect on GHG emissions reduction during the past years. The shift towards industrial pig production was easier to manage from the environmental perspective, and adequate large-scale cultivation was encouraged.
51: 4.1. Historical dynamics and the future estimate of GHG emissions from pig production in China

There has been a lot of literature indicating that livestock pro- duction is closely associated with GHG emissions (Savian et al., 2014). In this study, we found that the total GHG emissions from the Chinese pig sector has been 127.08 Mt CO2-eq in 2016. The value presented a slight downward percentage of 1.00% compared to that in 2000. Standing at the point of total GHG emissions, it could conclude that the pig production system in China did not show an obvious reduced effect on GHG during the past years. Nowadays, China has been one of the largest contributors to global GHG emissions. In 2017, the agricultural GHG emissions in China have been about 0.68 billion tons CO2-eq, ranking the first in the world (FAO, 2020). A previous study in our research group indicated that crop production pushes up GHG emissions in China during 2000-2016 (Wang et al., 2019a). The Chinese government prom- ised to reach the highest point of GHG emissions in 2030 through diversified technologies for reducing emissions. Therefore, Chinese agricultural systems, including crop and livestock production, should pay more attention to exploring GHG reduction practices.
Nevertheless, a fact must be noticed: the pig production quan- tity in China was increased by 35.12% during 2000-2016. In other words, the GHG emissions of producing unit pig in China were

significantly decreased in the past years. The primary reason causing the result was the changed pig rearing models. From 2000 to 2016, the pig production quantity from the BF decreased by 80.90%, while the quantities from the SF, MF and LF increased by 316.63%, 444.15%, 798.08%, respectively. Our results demonstrated that the pig's CF in the BF has been higher than other models in China during the research period, especially before 2004. Thus, the expansion of intensive pig production farms including the SF, MF and LF actually reflected the adoption of pig rearing models with the lower CFs in China. Similar viewpoints have been reported before. Crosson et al. (2011) indicated that intensification of pro- duction could reduce emissions/kg product when input re- quirements of feed and/or fertilizer were not excessive. Dourmad et al. (2014) demonstrated that the degree of intensification inversely correlated with the environmental impact per kg of live pig produced. Qian et al. (2018) indicated that the animals raised with large-scale feedlots practice generated fewer pollutants than those raised in households by investigating the environmental status of livestock and poultry sectors in China. Thus, the pig production scale in China would not show the fact of "the larger, the better".

The shift towards industrial pig production was easier to manage from the environmental perspective, and adequate large-scale cultivation was encouraged.

55: <header>4.2. CF comparison of unit pig in different cases </header>

In this study, we figured out that the average CF of unit pig in China was 1.72 kg CO2-eq/kg during 2000-2016. In order to compare the CFs of pig production in China with that in other countries, this paper compiled some results from published liter- atures. The obvious difference between the CF results for pig pro- duction could be viewed. For example, Reckmann et al. (2013) calculated that the pig production in Germany was 4.29 kg CO2- eq/kg. Noya et al. (2016) indicated a value of 6.09 kg CO2-eq/kg for pork production in Catalonia, Spain. Vergé et al. (2016) reported that the GHG emissions intensity of pig production in Canada was between 2.63 and 3.64 kg CO2-eq/kg. The CF values were higher than our results. The difference between the results were primarily derived from the inconsistent system boundaries of CF accounting of pig production system. Firstly, the system boundary in the pre- sent case considered five stages, including feedstuffs production,
Fig. 5. Distribution of stochastically calculated unit CF of different pig production models in China derived by Monte Carlo simulation.

feeds processing, enteric fermentation, farm management and manure management, while the system boundaries were extended from feedstuffs production to the slaughter and processing stages of pig in other studies. A few studies extended their system boundaries to slaughterhouse gate, reporting the unit CFs of pork with the higher values, but some papers indicated that the slaughtering-related contributions were negligible for pig produc- tion system. For example, Gonzalez-Garcia (2015) indicated that the slaughtering process only contributed to 2% of the total GWP of pig production. Noya et al. (2016) reported that the stages of slaughterhouse and pork cutting only made up to a small contri- bution to their results. Secondly, the GHG emissions from the transportation process during the whole life cycle of pig production were generally included from the studies but excluded in this study. Third, the rearing of sows, piglets and breeding boar were not taken into account in the research due to unavailable data. Actually, it should be noted that the pig breeding process leads to significant GHG emissions in actual production, especially for the medium and large-scale farms. Therefore, the CF results calculated in this study would be increased if relative datasets were available in the

59: At the same time, the majority published papers analyzed the GHG emissions of pig production system based on specific pig rearing models. For instance, Luo et al. (2015) reported that the CF of unit pig was 5.42 and 4.29 kg CO2-eq/kg in household and aggregated farms in Sichuan province in China, respectively. Zhou et al. (2018) reported that the CF of unit pig was 3.39 kg CO2-eq/ kg in Hebei province in China. The raw data adopted in their calculation were comprehensive. Rather, the raw data used in the present study were derived from several statistical yearbooks at national level, causing to the inevitable loss of data. Thus, the CF values in their researches were higher than those in our study. Moreover, different researches always used different CF ac- counting parameters, leading to the inconsistent calculation re- sults. For example, the 100-year GWP of N20 used in Luo et al. (2015) and Zhou et al. (2018) were 298, while the value used in this study was 265 according to the IPCC's fifth assessment report. The GHG emissions factors of electricity used in Zhou et al. (2018) and Luo et al. (2015) were 0.88 and 0.92 kg CO2-eq/kWh, respec- tively, which were higher than that in the present study (0.68 kg
Fig. 6. Distribution of stochastically calculated total GHG emissions of pig production system in China derived by Monte Carlo simulation.
CO2-eq/kWh). The lower accounting parameters possibly resulted in the lower CF result in this research.

Finally, different selections on allocation methods and func- tional unit during CF accounting might be partly responsible for the gap between the results in this study and in other researches. For example, Wiedemann et al. (2016) and Arrieta and González (2019) reported the CFs of pig production in Australian and Argentina based on economic and mass allocation approaches, respectively, while this study estimated the CF of Chinese pig production system by using the energy-based allocation. Some researches selected pig's carcass weight (Nguyen et al., 2010) or deboned pigmeat (Jacobsen et al., 2014) as functional units, while live weight was used as a functional unit in this study. Consequently, the various allocation methods and functional units adopted in different re- searches possibly contributed to the difference between the pig's CF results in the studies.

Overall, the inconsistent calculation approach and data sources caused to the various CF values of pig in these studies. Although the issues were hard to be avoided at present, a standardized CF ac- counting framework was expected in the future for the more ac- curate quantification of the GHG emissions from livestock production system.

CO2-eq/kWh). The lower accounting parameters possibly resulted in the lower CF result in this research.

Finally, different selections on allocation methods and func- tional unit during CF accounting might be partly responsible for the gap between the results in this study and in other researches. For example, Wiedemann et al. (2016) and Arrieta and González (2019) reported the CFs of pig production in Australian and Argentina based on economic and mass allocation approaches, respectively, while this study estimated the CF of Chinese pig production system by using the energy-based allocation. Some researches selected pig's carcass weight (Nguyen et al., 2010) or deboned pigmeat (Jacobsen et al., 2014) as functional units, while live weight was used as a functional unit in this study. Consequently, the various allocation methods and functional units adopted in different re- searches possibly contributed to the difference between the pig's CF results in the studies.

Overall, the inconsistent calculation approach and data sources caused to the various CF values of pig in these studies. Although the issues were hard to be avoided at present, a standardized CF ac- counting framework was expected in the future for the more ac- curate quantification of the GHG emissions from livestock production system.
62: The meat production quantity in China has been the top one in the world for more than twenty years (Li et al., 2016). The pig production system constituted of the primary pollution sources (Gan and Hu, 2016). The population growth and the increasing per capita consumption of pork are the primary factors influencing the demand for pig production in China. In this study, the scenarios A1 and A2 would rise at 127.80 (+3.56%) and 170.36 (+38.04%) Mt CO2- eq/yr in 2030, respectively. It implied that GHG emissions would rise continuously with the growth of population and per capita meat consumption in China. Rising demand for pork in China would result in the acute challenge in reducing GHG emissions in the future. Thus, sustained and effective mitigation strategies are ur- gently required to minimize GHG emissions derived from pig sector. Fortunately, our scenario analysis illustrated that the pig production system in China still showed considerable mitigation potential for GHG emissions.
In this study, we found that the feed crop cultivation was

regarded as the main process of GHG emissions in the Chinese pig production system, ranging from 34.28% to 47.18%. Many researches had shown that advanced agricultural measures could effectively reduce GHG emissions in farmland. For instance, synchronous optimization of nitrogen rates and types presented the obvious GHG mitigation effect in rice-wheat rotation system in Southeast China (Jiang et al., 2020). Conservation tillage practices had the potential to reduce GHG emissions in field through increased car- bon storage or reduced soil respiration rate (Dachraoui and Sombrero, 2020) and generated savings of fuel (Sorensen et al., 2014). Straw mulching increased soil CH4 absorption (Chen et al., 2017). Improving water management could reduce N20 and CH4 emissions (Wang et al., 2020b). Therefore, better environmental performance of pig production system in China could be obtained by adopting the practices above in feed crop production subsystems.

Optimizing pig feed composition should be taken into account as well. Lowering dietary crude protein content (Nakamura and Itsubo, 2019) and supplementation of protease (Pan et al., 2017) significantly decreased total N excretion and NH3 emissions (Wang et al., 2020a). Meanwhile, feed conversion rate was also a key point of the GHG emissions reduction strategy. According to the scenario analysis in this study, improving feed conversion rate could reduce GHG emissions by about 15.59 Mt CO2-eq/yr in 2030 compared to that in 2016. Cherubini et al. (2015) also demonstrated that the improving feed conversion rate could lower the CF for pig pro- duction by 11.7%. Thus, optimizing the feed composition for the improvement of feed conversion rate would be an effective approach for GHG emissions reduction of pig production in China.

In the present case, furthermore, manure management contributed to 15.07%-25.34% of GHG emissions in pig production system in China. The results implied that reasonable manure management measures could significantly reduce the GHG emis- sions of animal rearing systems. Scenarios analysis demonstrated that obvious substitution effects could be achieved by the imple- mentation of biogas projects, ranging from 18.48 to 49.63 Mt CO2- eq/yr. Actually, China government was popularizing the construc- tion of biogas project in the rural area (MOA, 2017). Developing biogas projects could optimize the energy consumption structure in China, showing remarkable prospects in relieving China's energy pressure and promoting environmental protection. Also, the po- tential of fecal and urine resource treatment to replace fertilizer was obvious (Wang et al., 2019b). Returning biogas residue to field has been an important practice for reducing agricultural GHG emissions (Yin et al., 2019). Thus, exploring improved manure management practice would be a significant way to reduce GHG emissions from pig production system in China.

Finally, the changes in Chinese diets would possibly affect GHG emissions derived from the pig sector in China. Pork consumption is still dominant in meat consumption in China nowadays. Cui and Wang (2016) reported that the per capita consumption of pork accounted for 65.98% of the total meat consumption from 2000 to 2014 in China while beef and mutton and poultry meat accounted for 9.33% and 6.5%, respectively. Compared to the Chinese diet structure, the diets in West countries were characterized by a high intake of meat, dairy products and eggs (Westhoek et al., 2014). The CF of beef was always ranged from 10.4 to 23.2 kg CO2-eq/kg (Huang et al., 2015), which was significantly higher the CF of pig production. Therefore, GHG emissions from the Chinese diet were usually lower than those in the West countries. However, China is developing into the most important market for ruminant products. The consumption of animal products with higher CF has been increasing rapidly in recent decades (Du et al., 2018). The changes in diet structure would possibly push up GHG emissions from China's agricultural system. In recent year, there were many

authors reported that switching into reduced-meat diets have a considerable impact on reducing GHG emissions (Scarborough et al., 2014; González et al., 2020). The GHG emissions of plant- based foods are normally lower of animal-based foods. Therefore, considering the increasing attention of Chinese to a healthy diet in the future, the GHG mitigation potential derived the change diet structure that could be expected in China.

In a word, the analysis above on the mitigation potential implied a fact: we should not just stand at the perspective of the separated pig production system to consider the GHG reduction measures. An integrated "soil-crop-livestock-food" agricultural industry chain involving the cleaner production strategies would be necessary for the anthropogenic GHG mitigation in pig production system in China.

An integrated "soil-crop-livestock-food" agricultural industry chain involving the cleaner production strategies would be necessary for the anthropogenic GHG mitigation in pig production system in China.
68: Uncertainty is an unavoidable issue in the assessment of GHG emissions of a production system at the national level. Although the MCS results have indicated that the parameters uncertainties only reflected a slight impact on the results, the correlations between parameters were not taken into account in this study. The neglect on the correlations might lead to new uncertainty (Bojacá and Schrevens, 2010). In this study, meanwhile, the raw data on the inputs and outputs of pig production in different models in China were obtained from the official statistical yearbook. The data about the annual output quantity of pig in different models in the year- books were partially missing, because the government updated the statistical method on the datasets after the third census on the agricultural sector in China. Although this study supplemented the lacked data by scientific estimation in the supplementary material (Table S11), the uncertainty affecting the calculation results has certainly existed. Finally, this study primarily selected default pa- rameters based on IPCC guidelines to calculate the GHG emissions during the enteric fermentation and manure management sub- system of pig production in China. Up to now, there are relatively few studies about enteric fermentation coefficient of pig intestines in China, giving the rise to unavailable local parameters. Detailed country-specific data rather than default vales were expected to be adopted into the calculation in the further research.

Furthermore, the system boundary in this study did extent to the slaughtering, processing and transportation of pork. In China, the process of pig slaughtering, processing and transportation showed great difference for the different rearing scales. In specific, some pigs in the BF and SF models would be slaughtered for their own con- sumption or purchased by others after finishing the feeding stage. Rather, the majority of pigs in the MF and LF models were directly slaughtered in the farms. The processing objectives and trans- portation of the pig from the models were significantly different as well. Consequently, the raw data on the inputs and outputs of pig slaughtering, processing and transportation were unavailable for this study if the system boundary of this study was extended to the slaughter or more processes. At the same time, this study ignored the effect of soybean international trade on the CFs of pig production in China. In actual production status, the soybean meal used in the Chinese livestock feedstock was primarily derived from the imported soybean from the United States, Brazil and Argentina. The crops planted in different regions present different CF results due to various natural and production conditions (Sun et al., 2018). Because the data details were unavailable, we could not know how much quantity of soybean was imported from the international market. If the information were considered, the quantification result of this present study was slightly changed. Of course, the more extensive system boundary and detailed data could help us have a more completed understanding on pig sector in China.

70: Developing low-carbon livestock production system has become an important way to cope with global climate change. This study introduced the CF method to analyze the GHG emissions derived from pig production system in China based on the national statistical data from 2000 to 2016. Results showed that the CF of pig production in China was showing a reduction trend. The average CF of unit mass alive pig in the BF, SF, MF and LF were 1.78 kg CO2-eq/ kg, 1.55 kg CO2-eq/kg, 1.65 kg CO2-eq/kg and 1.65 kg CO2-eq/kg, respectively, during 2000-2016. The total GHG emissions of the Chinese pig system were slightly decreased during the study period, while the pig production quantity was increased by 35.12%. It implied that the pig production system in China had developed toward a positive effect on GHG emissions reduction during the past years. At the same time, the results illustrated that the GHG emissions of pig production systems in China were mainly derived from feed cultivation and manure management. Based on scenario analysis, this study estimated that the Chinese pig sector still had considerable mitigation potential for GHG emissions. The shift to- wards intensive pig production, improved feed crop cultivation and manure management systems would be the key points to reduce the GHG emissions of pig production system. Considering the rapid growth of pork demand for Chinese, various advanced measures should be explored for reducing GHG emissions from pig produc- tion. This would contribute to realizing the GHG reduction target in China by 2030.
